name: Coll de la Gallina Loop
name_original: Subida al Coll de la Gallina con descenso vía Mirador de Canolich — circular desde Sant Julià de Lòria
description: Cycle the Coll de la Gallina Loop, a difficult 16.5-mile (26.5 km) road cycling route in Andorra with 3501 feet (1067 meters) of elevation g
region_intro: Road cycling in the Pyrenees offers a diverse landscape characterized by challenging mountain passes, deep-cut valleys, and extensive ridgelines. The region features sustained climbs on smooth pavement, with routes often winding through forested slopes and open alpine pastures. Cyclists encounter rugged peaks and dramatic scenery, making it a destination for varied skill levels. Best no traffic road cycling routes in the Pyrenees The most popular no traffic road cycling route is Hautacam – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees, a 40.9 miles (65.8 km) trail that takes 3 hours 55 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation gain through iconic mountain terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Loop 1: The Jaizkibel – Basque Country passes, a moderate 19.6 miles (31.6 km) path. This route explores the scenic passes of the Basque Country, offering varied gradients and coastal views. Local road cyclists also love the The mining route from Berga, a 77.9 miles (125.5 km) trail leading through historical landscapes and natural parks, often completed in about 7 hours 50 minutes. Road cycling in the Pyrenees is defined by iconic mountain passes, glacial lakes, and protected national parks, with options ranging from moderate climbs to difficult, high-elevation routes. The routes in Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 6,500 reviews. More than 375,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Pyrenees's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no-traffic road cycling routes are available in the Pyrenees?
A: There are over 950 no-traffic road cycling routes in the Pyrenees, offering a wide range of options for all skill levels. This includes more than 110 easy routes, nearly 500 moderate routes, and over 340 difficult routes for experienced cyclists.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on no-traffic road cycling routes in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees offer a diverse and challenging terrain, even on no-traffic routes. You can expect sustained climbs, thrilling descents, and smooth pavement. Routes often feature rugged peaks, deep-cut valleys, extensive ridgelines, and enjoyable hairpin bends through forested slopes. The region is famous for its iconic mountain passes, providing a true test of endurance with breathtaking views.

Q: Are there any family-friendly no-traffic road cycling routes in the Pyrenees?
A: While many Pyrenean routes are known for their challenging climbs, there are over 110 easy routes available that are suitable for families or those looking for a more relaxed ride away from traffic. These routes often feature gentler gradients and scenic paths through valleys or along quieter roads, allowing everyone to enjoy the stunning landscapes.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions I can see along these routes?
A: The Pyrenees are rich in natural wonders. You might encounter spectacular glacial formations, such as the UNESCO World Heritage site &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710757" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cirque de Gavarnie&lt;/a&gt; with its massive waterfall, or the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5186545" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lac d'Oô&lt;/a&gt;, also featuring an impressive waterfall. Other highlights include the numerous high-altitude lakes in the Néouvielle Nature Reserve, or the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/151545" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte Perdido&lt;/a&gt; peak.

Q: Are there any circular no-traffic road cycling routes in the Pyrenees?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the difficult route "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e943713854/serra-d-ensija-circular-tour-of-the-cadi-moixero-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Serra d'Ensija - Circular Tour of the Cadí Moixeró Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;" offers a challenging circular experience through stunning scenery. You can find various circular options across different difficulty levels.

Q: What is the best time of year for no-traffic road cycling in the Pyrenees?
A: The best time for road cycling in the Pyrenees is typically from late spring (May/June) through early autumn (September/October). During these months, the weather is generally more stable, and most of the high mountain passes are clear of snow. Summer offers warmer temperatures, while spring and autumn provide cooler conditions and often fewer crowds, ideal for enjoying the quiet roads.

Q: What do other cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.55 stars. Cyclists frequently praise the region's challenging climbs, the thrill of descending iconic passes, and the truly stunning, untouched natural scenery. The absence of traffic on these specific routes allows for a more immersive and peaceful riding experience.

Q: Are there any routes that feature famous mountain passes?
A: Absolutely. The Pyrenees are home to many legendary cols, frequently featured in major cycling races. While specific no-traffic routes might vary, you can find routes that incorporate or lead to areas near famous passes. For instance, the difficult route "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064436547/hautacam-emblematic-passes-of-the-pyrenees" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hautacam – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees&lt;/a&gt;" takes you through high mountain passes, offering a taste of these iconic climbs.

Q: Can I find routes with significant elevation gain for a challenging ride?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees are renowned for their significant elevation gains, even on no-traffic routes. Many difficult routes feature thousands of meters of climbing. For example, "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1350100752/the-mining-route-from-berga" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The mining route from Berga&lt;/a&gt;" involves nearly 3,500 meters of ascent, providing a substantial challenge for experienced road cyclists.

Q: Are there any coastal no-traffic road cycling options in the Pyrenees?
A: While the core Pyrenees are mountainous, the range extends to the sea in areas like the Basque Corniche. Here, you can find routes that offer a unique blend of mountain scenery and coastal paths, providing varied views and a different cycling experience. These routes often feature less traffic and stunning sea vistas.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I spot on these routes?
A: The protected areas of the Pyrenees, such as the Pyrenees National Park, are havens for wildlife. While cycling, especially on quieter, no-traffic routes, you might have the chance to spot various species. Keep an eye out for ibex, chamois, and a wide array of birdlife. Brown bears are present but rarely seen.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for beginners looking for no-traffic road cycling?
A: Yes, beginners can find suitable no-traffic road cycling routes. With over 110 easy routes available, you can choose options with gentler gradients and shorter distances. These routes provide an excellent opportunity to enjoy the Pyrenean scenery and build confidence without the challenge of heavy traffic or extreme climbs. Look for routes labeled 'easy' to start your adventure.
tips: An ancient mountain pass links the communities of this mountainous region. At 1910 meters above sea level, the pass offers spectacular views of the surrounding landscape and the valley.
Walk to the Sanctuary of the Virgin of Canòlich. The route from Aixovall leaves the dense forest to offer you panoramic views of the valley.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Coll de la Gallina Loop for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This loop is considered difficult, demanding very good fitness. The climb from Sant Julià de Lòria is approximately 11.4 to 12.2 km long with over 1000 meters of elevation gain and an average gradient of 8.4% to 9.1%. You'll encounter numerous pitches above 15%, with some sections reaching an 'inhuman' 18-20%.

faqs.question: What is the terrain and road surface like on this route?
faqs.answer: The road surface on the Coll de la Gallina is generally in excellent condition, often described as having 'perfect tarmac,' especially after resurfacing for cycling events. The ascent involves consistently high gradients and winding roads through fir trees. The descent, particularly through the sanctuary side, features technical hairpin turns.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Coll de la Gallina Loop?
faqs.answer: As you cycle this route, you'll pass the iconic Coll de la Gallina mountain pass itself. The descent also takes you past the scenic Sanctuary of Canòlic Viewpoint, offering breathtaking panoramas.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Coll de la Gallina Loop?
faqs.answer: The loop typically starts from Sant Julià de Lòria. You should be able to find parking facilities within the town to begin your ride. Given the minimal traffic on the climb itself, parking in the town provides a convenient starting point.

faqs.question: Is the Coll de la Gallina Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is categorized as 'difficult' and requires 'very good fitness.' With sustained steep gradients and technical descents, it is not recommended for beginner road cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle the Coll de la Gallina Loop is generally from late spring to early autumn (May to October) when the weather is more stable and the mountain passes are clear of snow. Always check local weather conditions before heading out, as mountain weather can change rapidly.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route partially overlaps with sections of other trails. Notably, it covers significant portions of the [CIMA AN02] Coll de la Gallina * Fontaneda and [CIMA AN02] Coll de la Gallina * Aixovall / Bixessarri routes. Smaller sections also coincide with the GRP La volta a Andorra and El Camí IT1TR5 El Camí Capçaler: de Salzes a Balaguer.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Coll de la Gallina Loop?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Coll de la Gallina Loop. The roads are public and freely accessible.

faqs.question: Can I bring my dog on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is a challenging road cycling route with significant traffic (albeit minimal for a mountain pass), it is generally not suitable or safe for dogs to accompany cyclists on the road itself. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular adventure.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: While the very summit might be a slight anticlimax in terms of panoramic views, the overall climb offers magnificent and magical scenery. You'll be treated to breathtaking panoramas of Andorra and the surrounding Pyrenees, with the road winding through picturesque fir trees, especially beautiful on the lower slopes.

faqs.question: What makes the Coll de la Gallina a legendary climb in the cycling world?
faqs.answer: The Coll de la Gallina is legendary due to its inclusion in prestigious professional races like La Vuelta a España, where it gained a reputation for being exceptionally challenging and 'broke the legs' of many contenders. Its relentless steepness and high altitude (1910 meters) contribute to its formidable status.
